title: Crown of Turkey
subtitle: Just the breast joint, roasted in under two hours with a sage butter and a bacon blanket
excerpt: A turkey crown is the breast section with the legs removed, sold on or off the bone. It roasts in half the time of a whole bird, with none of the timing anxiety of juggling breast and leg. Eight people are served from a 2.5 kg crown without waste.
sourceNotes: Delia Smith, Delia's Happy Christmas (2009): turkey crown roasting guide. Nigella Lawson, Feast (2004): sage and bacon-covered crown. British supermarket-driven popularisation from the 1990s onwards.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sage butter can be made and pushed under the skin up to 24 hours ahead. Cover and refrigerate; bring to room temperature 1 hour before roasting.
recipe.temperatureNote: fan oven throughout; remove bacon for final 15 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: A turkey with the legs and wings removed, leaving just the breast section on the carcass bones or fully boned and rolled. It roasts faster and more evenly than a whole bird, producing uniformly moist white meat.

Laying strips of fat bacon or thin sheets of fat over the surface of lean meat or poultry before roasting, to baste the joint as the fat renders. Commonly used on turkey crowns and game birds to prevent the lean surface from drying.

content.text: eliminates the chief problem of whole-bird roasting: the legs needing far more cooking than the breast. With the legs gone, the roasting time shortens to around 1 hour 45 minutes for a 2.5 kg crown, and the internal temperature at which you pull it is a consistent 70-72°C throughout.
Sage butter under the skin seasons the flesh and keeps it moist during the long dry heat. Streaky bacon laid in overlapping strips over the breast is
: it self-bastes the crown as it renders, and the rashers themselves are a cook's perk when they come off crispily at the end.
Heat the oven to 190°C (170°C fan). Beat the

. Ease the skin away from the breast and push two thirds of the butter mixture under the skin, spreading it across both lobes. Rub the remaining butter over the outside of the skin. Lay the bacon rashers in overlapping strips across the top of the crown.

Set the crown in the roasting pan and roast for 1 hour 30 minutes. Remove the bacon rashers and return the crown to the oven for a final 15 minutes to colour the skin. The crown is done when a probe at the thickest part of the breast reads 70-72°C. Transfer to a warm board and rest for 20 minutes; the temperature will carry over by another 3-4°C during resting.
Pour off excess fat from the pan. Add
if using and scrape the fond over medium heat. Add
and simmer for 5 minutes. Season and strain into a warm jug. Carve the crown at the table in long, even slices across the grain.

rows.symptom: Skin is pale after 1 hour 45 minutes
rows.cause: Bacon was left on too long, or oven temperature was lower than expected
rows.fix: Remove the bacon earlier, at the 1 hour 15 minute mark. Check oven temperature with a thermometer.

rows.symptom: Meat reads 70°C at 1 hour but you expected longer
rows.cause: Crown was closer to 3 kg and cooked faster than 2.5 kg
rows.fix: Probe at 1 hour and adjust. There is no minimum time, only a temperature to reach. Pull it when the probe is right.

rows.cause: Overcooked past 75°C, or the crown sat unrested for too long
rows.fix: Pull at 70-72°C and rest promptly. Serve with plenty of gravy and stuffing to compensate.

rows.symptom: Butter under the skin has leaked into the pan and burned
rows.cause: Too much butter was pushed under the skin, or the crown was on too high a heat
rows.fix: Two thirds under the skin, one third on the outside is the right ratio. A small amount of fond in the pan is fine; charred solid residue means the heat was too high.

content.text: The turkey crown became Britain's default Christmas centrepiece for smaller households once supermarkets began selling them widely in the 1990s. It suits the household that wants the full roast experience without the size, the timing complexity, or the days of leftover turkey that follow a whole 7 kg bird. Eight people are fed comfortably, and the leftovers are manageable.
